result:
I keep thinking about the quiet spacecraft that passes over my neighborhood every seventh night. It hums like an old refrigerator waking up, gliding just above the chimneys, close enough to snag stray socks off clotheslines if it wanted. No one else seems to notice—dogs don’t bark, porch lights do not flicker, and the local news remains obsessed with potholes. I wave anyway, half-hoping its pilot might mistake me for a volunteer ambassador of Earth.  

Tonight the craft hesitated, tilting an invisible wing. For a breath, gravity felt negotiable; my camellia bush leaned thirty degrees too far, blossoms clinging like dazzled trapeze artists. A slip of silver paper fluttered down—not foil or Mylar, but something between liquid and light. It landed on the sidewalk, spelling a single shimmering word before dissolving: “Listen.”  

So I stand barefoot now in the middle of the street, cellphone dim, heart loud. At first I only hear the undertow of suburbia: an air-conditioner sigh, a car-door thunk, someone practicing trumpet behind closed blinds. But under it all thrums a subtler sound, the rhythm of engines I forgot I carried—kidneys purring, blood in soft parade, wishbones vibrating like tuning forks. The craft waits, patient propeller in the sky. It doesn’t want worship or fear; it’s just asking me to keep the appointment I made before birth, to hear the tiny cosmos I cart around inside my ribs. Somewhere, a dog finally barks. The spacecraft unslings its next sentence of starlight.

I keep thinking about the quiet spacecraft that passes over my neighborhood every seventh night. It hums like an old refrigerator waking up, gliding just above the chimneys, close enough to snag stray socks off clotheslines if it wanted. No one else seems to notice—dogs don’t bark, porch lights do not flicker, and the local news remains obsessed with potholes. I wave anyway, half-hoping its pilot might mistake me for a volunteer ambassador of Earth.  

Tonight the craft hesitated, tilting an invisible wing. For a breath, gravity felt negotiable; my camellia bush leaned thirty degrees too far, blossoms clinging like dazzled trapeze artists. A slip of silver paper fluttered down—not foil or Mylar, but something between liquid and light. It landed on the sidewalk, spelling a single shimmering word before dissolving: “Listen.”  

So I stand barefoot now in the middle of the street, cellphone dim, heart loud. At first I only hear the undertow of suburbia: an air-conditioner sigh, a car-door thunk, someone practicing trumpet behind closed blinds. But under it all thrums a subtler sound, the rhythm of engines I forgot I carried—kidneys purring, blood in soft parade, wishbones vibrating like tuning forks. The craft waits, patient propeller in the sky. It doesn’t want worship or fear; it’s just asking me to keep the appointment I made before birth, to hear the tiny cosmos I cart around inside my ribs. Somewhere, a dog finally barks. The spacecraft unslings its next sentence of starlight.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
